steps.title: Spot the key signal
steps.content: The claim asks for evidence that Newland compares his intellect “favorably” to other men — this requires a quotation showing direct intellectual superiority with specific reasons.

steps.title: Apply the signal to the choices
steps.content: Choice C explicitly states Newland “felt himself distinctly the superior” and provides concrete intellectual justification: “had probably read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world.” The other three describe physical movements, discuss social etiquette, or show him accepting others' moral views despite viewing them individually as inferior — none illustrate the favorable intellectual comparison the claim requires. That matches choice C — C is correct.
